§743(b) adjustment = Outside basis - Share of inside basis
Allocate adjustment to assets based on relative appreciation
Adjustment is personal to the transferee partner

tbs.title: Qualified Small Business Stock (§1202)
tbs.scenario:
Jennifer acquired stock in TechStart Inc. (C corporation) on January 15, Year 1:
• Acquisition: Original issue from corporation
• Purchase price: $500,000
• TechStart's gross assets at all times: Under $50 million

Jennifer sells the stock on March 20, Year 6:
• Sale price: $3,000,000
• Corporation was active business (technology) throughout holding period

Jennifer's other capital gains: $200,000 (LTCG from other sales)
Jennifer's taxable income: $450,000 (single)

requirements.question: Calculate the gain on sale.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Sale price $3,000,000 - Basis $500,000 = $2,500,000 gain.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Is the stock Qualified Small Business Stock (QSBS)?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

QSBS requirements met: (1) C corporation, (2) acquired at original issuance, (3) gross assets < $50M, (4) active business, (5) held > 5 years (Jan Y1 to Mar Y6 = 5+ years).

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the maximum §1202 exclusion.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

For stock acquired after 9/27/2010, exclusion = 100% of gain (up to greater of $10M or 10× basis). Maximum = $10M or $5M (10 × $500K). Gain of $2.5M is fully excludable.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate Jennifer's taxable QSBS gain.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

100% exclusion applies. $2,500,000 × 100% = $2,500,000 excluded. Taxable = $0.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate federal tax on the QSBS sale (ignoring other income).
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

No federal tax on excluded QSBS gain. The 100% exclusion results in $0 tax. (Note: Some states do not conform.)

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: QSBS: C corp, < $50M assets, original issue, active business, 5+ year hold
100% exclusion for stock acquired after 9/27/2010
Exclusion limit: Greater of $10M or 10× adjusted basis
7% AMT preference eliminated for 100% exclusion stock

tbs.title: Entity Selection Comparison
tbs.scenario:
Client is starting a consulting business expecting:
• Year 1 net income: $200,000
• Self-employment tax concerns
• Plans to retain 50% of profits for growth
• Two equal 50% owners (both high-income)
• May seek venture capital in 3-5 years

Consider: Sole proprietorship, Partnership (LLC), S Corporation, C Corporation

requirements.question: Which entity best addresses self-employment tax concerns?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

S Corporation. Owner-employees receive salary (subject to FICA) and distributions (not subject to SE/FICA). "Reasonable compensation" is required, but remaining profits escape SE tax.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Which entity has the biggest disadvantage for retaining profits?
options: S Corporation - phantom income to owners
C Corporation - accumulated earnings tax risk
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

S Corporation (and partnership). Pass-through entities tax owners on income whether distributed or not ("phantom income"). Retained profits still increase owner taxable income.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Which entity is most attractive for venture capital investment?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

C Corporation. VCs prefer C corps: (1) no pass-through income to tax-exempt investors, (2) can issue preferred stock, (3) easier path to IPO, (4) familiar structure.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: What is the recommended initial structure?
options: Start as S Corp, convert to C Corp for VC
Start as C Corp from day one
Start as LLC taxed as partnership
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

S Corp initially provides self-employment tax savings. Converting to C Corp before VC investment is straightforward. This approach optimizes early tax savings while maintaining flexibility.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: SE tax: Sole prop and partnership = all income; S corp = wages only
Retained earnings: Pass-throughs have phantom income issue
VC investment: C corps strongly preferred
Conversion: S to C is easy; C to S may trigger BIG tax

tbs.title: Charitable Remainder Trust Planning
tbs.scenario:
Margaret (age 68) transfers appreciated stock to a Charitable Remainder Unitrust (CRUT):

Transfer:
• Stock FMV: $1,000,000
• Margaret's basis: $200,000

CRUT Terms:
• 6% annual payout to Margaret for life
• Remainder to charity at death
• §7520 rate: 4%

IRS factor for 6% CRUT, age 68: 0.35 (35% present value of remainder)

requirements.question: Calculate the charitable deduction for the transfer.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

FMV × PV factor = $1,000,000 × 0.35 = $350,000 charitable deduction.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the first year unitrust payment.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

FMV × payout rate = $1,000,000 × 6% = $60,000 (Year 1, assuming no change in trust value).

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: When Margaret transfers the stock to the trust, does she recognize gain?
options: No - gain deferred until payments received
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

No immediate gain recognition. The gain ($800,000) is deferred and recognized proportionally as Margaret receives unitrust payments. The trust can sell the stock without triggering tax.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: How are unitrust payments taxed to Margaret?
options: Four-tier system based on trust income
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Four-tier system: (1) Ordinary income first, (2) Capital gains, (3) Other income, (4) Return of principal. Payments carry out income in tier order.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: If the trust sells the stock for $1,000,000, what is the trust's capital gain?
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Trust takes carryover basis ($200,000). Sale at $1,000,000 = $800,000 LTCG. The gain remains in the trust (tax-exempt entity) and is passed out to Margaret under the four-tier rules.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: CRT = Tax-exempt trust (no tax on sale of appreciated assets)
Deduction = FMV × PV factor for remainder interest
Four-tier taxation: Ordinary → Capital gain → Other → Principal
CRUT payout varies with trust value; CRAT is fixed

tbs.title: Installment Sale Reporting
tbs.scenario:
Robert sells investment land under an installment sale:

Sale Details:
• Sale price: $500,000
• Robert's adjusted basis: $200,000
• Selling expenses: $20,000
• Down payment received: $100,000
• Note receivable: $400,000 (8% interest, 4 annual payments)

Year 1: Received down payment + first installment of $100,000 (plus $32,000 interest)
Year 2: Received second installment of $100,000 (plus $24,000 interest)

requirements.question: Calculate the gross profit.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Sale price $500,000 - Basis $200,000 - Selling expenses $20,000 = $280,000 gross profit.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the gross profit ratio.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Gross profit / Contract price = $280,000 / $500,000 = 56%

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate Year 1 recognized gain.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Payments received × GP ratio = ($100,000 + $100,000) × 56% = $112,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate Year 1 interest income.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Interest is reported separately from installment gain: $32,000 ordinary interest income.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate Year 2 recognized gain.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Year 2 principal payment $100,000 × 56% = $56,000 capital gain.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Gross profit = Sale price - Adjusted basis - Selling costs
GP ratio = Gross profit / Contract price
Each payment: Principal × GP ratio = Recognized gain
Interest is ordinary income, separate from installment gain

tbs.title: Tax Penalty Avoidance
tbs.scenario:
Review these client situations for potential penalties:

Client A: Individual estimated $50,000 tax liability. Prior year tax was $15,000. Client pays $15,000 in estimated taxes throughout year.

Client B: S Corporation fails to file Form 1120-S by March 15 (no extension). Files on June 1. Has 4 shareholders.

Client C: Corporation underpays estimated taxes by $100,000 for the year. The underpayment was due to a casualty loss that occurred in Q4.

Client D: Individual takes aggressive position on return that has 30% likelihood of success. No disclosure made.

requirements.question: Does Client A owe an underpayment penalty?
options: No - met 100% of prior year safe harbor
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

No penalty. Client paid 100% of prior year tax ($15,000). This meets the safe harbor. (If prior year AGI > $150,000, need 110% of prior year.)

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate Client B's late filing penalty for S Corporation.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Penalty = $220 per shareholder per month (or fraction). 4 shareholders × $220 × 3 months (Mar 16-Jun 1 = ~2.5 months rounded up) = approximately $2,640-$2,860.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Can Client C use the annualized income method to reduce penalty?
options: Yes - if income was lower in early quarters
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Yes. If the casualty loss occurred in Q4, taxable income was higher in earlier quarters. Annualized income installment method may reduce or eliminate the underpayment penalty.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: What penalty exposure does Client D have?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Accuracy-related penalty (20%) for substantial understatement. A 30% likelihood position does not meet "substantial authority" (generally 40%+) threshold. Disclosure with reasonable basis could avoid penalty.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Individual safe harbors: 90% current year OR 100%/110% prior year
S Corp late filing: $220/shareholder/month
Annualized income method helps when income bunches late
Substantial authority (~40%) avoids penalty; reasonable basis (20%) with disclosure

tbs.title: Partnership Liquidating Distribution
tbs.scenario:
ABC Partnership is liquidating. Partner A's situation:

Partner A's Information:
• Outside basis before distribution: $120,000
• Share of partnership liabilities assumed by others: $30,000

Liquidating Distribution to Partner A:
• Cash: $25,000
• Inventory (partnership basis $20,000, FMV $35,000)
• Land (partnership basis $40,000, FMV $55,000)
• Equipment (partnership basis $15,000, FMV $18,000)

No hot assets (§751) apply.
The partnership has no §704(c) built-in gains attributable to Partner A.

requirements.question: Calculate Partner A's outside basis after liability relief.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Outside basis = $120,000 - $30,000 liability relief = $90,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate remaining basis after cash distribution.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Remaining basis = $90,000 - $25,000 cash = $65,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate Partner A's basis in the inventory received.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

In liquidating distribution, inventory takes carryover basis ($20,000) since sufficient outside basis remains.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate Partner A's basis in the land received.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

After cash ($25K) and inventory ($20K), remaining basis = $45,000. Allocated between land and equipment. Land share = $45,000 × ($40K/($40K+$15K)) = $45,000 × 72.7% = $32,727. Actually, remaining $45,000 splits by relative basis.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Liquidating distributions: substituted basis
Cash first, then inventory at carryover basis
Remaining basis allocated to other property

tbs.title: S Corporation Built-In Gains Tax
tbs.scenario:
Omega Corp. converted from C corporation to S corporation on January 1, Year 1.

Assets at conversion date (Year 1):
| Asset | FMV at Conversion | C Corp Basis |
|-------|-------------------|--------------|
| Inventory | $200,000 | $150,000 |
| Equipment | $300,000 | $180,000 |
| Land | $500,000 | $350,000 |
| Building | $400,000 | $280,000 |

Activity in Year 3 (within recognition period):
• Sold equipment for $290,000 (adjusted basis at sale: $150,000)
• Sold land for $520,000

Taxable income before BIG tax: $180,000
Corporate tax rate: 21%

requirements.question: Calculate recognized built-in gain on equipment sale.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Conversion BIG = $300,000 - $180,000 = $120,000. Gain on sale = $290,000 - $150,000 = $140,000. Recognized BIG = lesser of gain ($140K) or conversion BIG ($120K) = $120,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate recognized built-in gain on land sale.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Conversion BIG = $500,000 - $350,000 = $150,000. Actual gain = $520,000 - $350,000 = $170,000. Recognized BIG = lesser = $150,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate total net recognized built-in gain.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Total NRBIG = Equipment BIG $120,000 + Land BIG $150,000 = $270,000. Limited to taxable income if lower.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate built-in gains tax liability.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

BIG tax = NRBIG (limited to taxable income) × 21% = $180,000 × 21% = $37,800 (taxable income is the limit)

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: BIG recognition period: 5 years from S election
BIG limited to gain existing at conversion
NRBIG limited to taxable income

tbs.title: Corporate Reorganization - Type A
tbs.scenario:
Target Corp. merges into Acquirer Corp. in a Type A reorganization.

Target Corp. shareholders:
Total shares: 10,000 shares
Aggregate adjusted basis: $2,000,000
Fair market value of Target shares: $5,000,000

Consideration from Acquirer Corp.:
• Acquirer stock: $4,000,000 FMV (80%)

requirements.question: Does this transaction qualify as a tax-free reorganization?

requirements.explanation:

80% stock satisfies continuity of interest (COI) requirement. Type A requires substantially all stock consideration. 80% generally meets this threshold.

requirements.question: Calculate Shareholder X's realized gain.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Realized gain = FMV received ($40,000 + $10,000) - Adjusted basis ($20,000) = $50,000 - $20,000 = $30,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate Shareholder X's recognized gain.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Recognized gain = Lesser of (realized gain $30,000) or (boot received $10,000) = $10,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate Shareholder X's basis in Acquirer stock.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Basis in stock = Old basis ($20,000) - Boot received ($10,000) + Gain recognized ($10,000) = $20,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Boot = non-stock consideration (cash, property)
Gain recognized limited to boot received
Basis: exchanged basis - boot + gain recognized

tbs.title: Estate Tax Calculation
tbs.scenario:
Decedent died in 2024. Estate information:

Gross Estate:
• Residence: $1,500,000
• Investment portfolio: $4,000,000

• Administration expenses: $150,000
• Funeral expenses: $25,000
• Debts owed at death: $75,000
• Charitable bequest: $500,000
• Marital deduction (QTIP trust): $3,000,000

Prior taxable gifts (post-1976): $500,000
Gift tax paid on prior gifts: $0 (within unified credit)
Applicable exclusion amount (2024): $13,610,000

requirements.question: Calculate gross estate.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Gross estate = $1,500,000 + $4,000,000 + $2,000,000 + $1,200,000 + $300,000 = $9,000,000

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate total deductions.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Deductions = Mortgage $400K + Admin $150K + Funeral $25K + Debts $75K + Charitable $500K + Marital $3,000K = $4,150,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate taxable estate.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Taxable estate = Gross estate $9,000,000 - Deductions $4,150,000 = $4,850,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate estate tax due (if any).
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Tax base = Taxable estate $4,850,000 + Prior gifts $500,000 = $5,350,000. This is below $13,610,000 exclusion, so no tax due.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Gross estate includes all assets at FMV at death
Marital and charitable deductions are unlimited
Add back post-1976 gifts for unified tax calculation

requirements.question: MegaCorp is subject to CAMT because:

CAMT applies to corporations with 3-year average adjusted financial statement income (AFSI) exceeding $1 billion.

requirements.question: Calculate adjusted financial statement income (AFSI).

requirements.question: Calculate tentative minimum tax (15% of AFSI).

tbs.title: Installment Sale with Related Party
tbs.scenario:
Parent sells rental property to Son (related party) under installment sale:

Property Information:

requirements.question: Calculate gross profit percentage.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Gross profit = $500,000 - $200,000 = $300,000. GP% = $300,000 / $500,000 = 60%

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate Year 1 gain recognized by Parent on payments received.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Depreciation recapture $50,000 recognized in year of sale. Installment gain: ($100,000 + $60,000) × 60% = $96,000. Total = $50,000 + $96,000 = $146,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: When Son sells to unrelated party, Parent must recognize:
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Related party disposition rule: Parent must recognize remaining deferred gain. Unpaid balance $280,000 × 60% = $168,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: The related party disposition rule applies because:
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

§453(e) triggers when related party disposes of property within 2 years. Son is related party and sold within 2 years.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Depreciation recapture recognized immediately

tbs.title: Tax Credits - R&D and Work Opportunity
tbs.scenario:
TechStart Inc. (qualified small business) tax credits for current year:

Research and Development Credit:

requirements.question: Calculate R&D credit using simplified method (14% of excess).
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Simplified: 14% × (Current QRE - 50% of 3-year average). 14% × ($500,000 - $175,000) = 14% × $325,000 = $45,500. Or alternative: 6% of QRE = $30,000.

Long-term unemployed: 40% of first $6,000 wages = $2,400 each × 3 = $7,200. Wait - special LTU rules: 40% of $10,000 = $4,000 each × 3 = $12,000. Actually: $18,000 × 40% = $7,200 per employee × 3 = $21,600 (capped appropriately).

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.explanation:

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: R&D credit: Regular method or simplified alternative

Loan repayment is return of basis if debt basis exists. Ending debt basis $10,000 shows no gain on $15,000 repayment.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: S corp basis: Stock basis first, then debt basis
Distributions reduce stock basis first
Debt repayment: Gain if exceeds debt basis

tbs.title: Net Operating Loss Carryforward
tbs.scenario:
CycliCorp has the following history:

Prior Year NOLs:
• Year 1: $500,000 NOL generated
• Year 2: $300,000 taxable income (before NOL)
• Year 3: $200,000 NOL generated

Current Year (Year 4):
• Gross income: $1,200,000
• Deductions: $400,000
• Taxable income before NOL: $800,000

Assume post-2017 NOL rules apply (no carryback, 80% limitation).
Corporate tax rate: 21%

requirements.question: Calculate NOL carryforward available at beginning of Year 4.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Year 1 NOL $500,000 - Used in Y2 (80% × $300,000 = $240,000) = $260,000. Plus Year 3 NOL $200,000 = $460,000 total

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate maximum NOL deduction for Year 4 (80% limit).
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

80% of taxable income before NOL = 80% × $800,000 = $640,000 maximum

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate actual NOL used in Year 4.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Lesser of available NOL ($460,000) or 80% limit ($640,000) = $460,000 used

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate Year 4 tax liability.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Taxable income = $800,000 - $460,000 NOL = $340,000. Tax = $340,000 × 21% = $71,400

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Post-2017 NOLs: No carryback (generally), 80% limitation
NOLs carried forward indefinitely
Use oldest NOLs first

tbs.title: International Tax - Foreign Tax Credit
tbs.scenario:
GlobalCorp (US corporation) has worldwide operations:

Worldwide Income:
• US source income: $2,000,000
• Foreign source income (general category): $800,000
• Foreign source income (passive category): $200,000

Foreign Taxes Paid:
• General category: $280,000
• Passive category: $50,000

US Tax Calculation:
• Pre-credit US tax: $630,000 (21% × $3,000,000)
• US tax rate: 21%

requirements.question: Calculate FTC limitation for general category.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

FTC limit = Pre-credit tax × (Foreign source income / Worldwide income) = $630,000 × ($800,000 / $3,000,000) = $168,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate allowable FTC for general category.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Lesser of taxes paid ($280,000) or limit ($168,000) = $168,000. Excess $112,000 carried forward.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate FTC limitation for passive category.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

FTC limit = $630,000 × ($200,000 / $3,000,000) = $42,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate total FTC and net US tax.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Total FTC = General $168,000 + Passive $42,000 = $210,000. Net tax = $630,000 - $210,000 = $420,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: FTC calculated separately by category
Limit = US tax × (Foreign income / Worldwide income)
Excess credits carried back 1 year or forward 10 years

tbs.title: Entity Selection Analysis
tbs.scenario:
New business venture with the following characteristics:

Business Profile:
• Expected Year 1 loss: $200,000
• Expected Year 2-5 income: $150,000 annually
• Equipment purchases Year 1: $100,000
• 3 owners (unrelated, all US citizens)
• Plan to reinvest all profits for 5 years
• Eventual sale or IPO contemplated

Owner Tax Situations:
• Owner A: AGI $300,000, 37% bracket, wants loss deductions
• Owner B: AGI $100,000, 24% bracket, passive investor, capital available
• Owner C: AGI $50,000, 12% bracket, active in business

Other Considerations:
• Venture capital possible in Year 3
• Need to attract key employees with equity
• Some owners prefer pass-through; others prefer limited liability
• State has no income tax

requirements.question: Which entity best allows Year 1 loss pass-through to owners?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Partnership/LLC offers most flexibility. S corp has at-risk and basis limitations. Owner B as passive may have PAL limits in both.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: The venture capital consideration suggests:
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

VC funds often require C corporation for: multiple stock classes, no shareholder restrictions, QSBS treatment potential.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: For key employee equity compensation, which is most flexible?
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

C corporations offer ISOs, NSOs, restricted stock with well-established rules. S corps limited in equity types. Partnerships have complex comp arrangements.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Owner B's investment will likely result in:
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Owner B is passive investor - PAL rules limit deductions to passive income. Suspended losses carry forward until disposition.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: S corp: 100 shareholders, one class stock, no nonresidents
C corp: Double taxation but more flexible for investment
LLC: Pass-through with flexible allocations

tbs.title: Form 709 - Gift Tax Return
tbs.scenario:
Richard Williams made the following gifts during 2024:

Gift 1: Cash gift to son
• Amount: $50,000
• Date: March 15, 2024
• Donee: Thomas Williams (son, age 35)
• Split gift election: Yes (with spouse consent)

Gift 2: Stock gift to daughter
• FMV on date of gift: $75,000
• Donor's adjusted basis: $30,000
• Date: July 1, 2024
• Donee: Elizabeth Williams (daughter, age 32)
• Split gift election: Yes (with spouse consent)

Gift 3: Tuition paid directly to university
• Amount: $45,000
• Paid directly to: State University
• For: Grandchild's tuition

Gift 4: 529 Plan contribution
• Amount: $90,000 (5-year election made)
• Date: December 1, 2024
• Beneficiary: Grandchild (age 10)

Additional Information:
• Richard is married to Margaret Williams
• Both consent to split all gifts
• No prior taxable gifts
• 2024 annual exclusion: $18,000 per donee

requirements.question: What is the total value of gifts reportable on Form 709 (before exclusions)?
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Cash $50,000 + Stock $75,000 + 529 $90,000 = $215,000. Tuition paid directly to school is excluded per §2503(e).

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: After gift splitting, what is Richard's share of reportable gifts (before annual exclusion)?
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

With split gifts: Cash $25,000 + Stock $37,500 + 529 $45,000 = $107,500 (each spouse)

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: For the 529 plan using the 5-year election, how much is excluded per year for Richard?
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Richard's share $45,000 ÷ 5 years = $9,000/year, but limited to $18,000 annual exclusion. Since $9,000 < $18,000, the entire $9,000 is excluded.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate Richard's taxable gifts for 2024.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Gift 1: $25,000 - $18,000 = $7,000 taxable. Gift 2: $37,500 - $18,000 = $19,500 taxable. Gift 4 (yr 1): $9,000 - $9,000 = $0 taxable. Total: $26,500

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Why is the $45,000 tuition payment not reported on Form 709?
options: It is below the annual exclusion
It qualifies for the educational exclusion when paid directly to the institution
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Payments made directly to an educational institution for tuition are excluded from gift tax under §2503(e), regardless of amount.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Direct tuition and medical payments to providers are unlimited exclusions
Gift splitting allows married couples to use both annual exclusions
529 plan contributions can use 5-year averaging for annual exclusion

tbs.title: Schedule K-1 Analysis - S Corporation
tbs.scenario:
You received a Schedule K-1 (Form 1120S) from Westbrook Manufacturing, Inc., an S corporation where you own 25% of the shares. Analyze the K-1 and determine the tax implications.

Schedule K-1 Information (Your Share - 25%):
Box 1 - Ordinary business income: $85,000
Box 2 - Net rental real estate income: $12,000
Box 4 - Interest income: $3,200
Box 5a - Ordinary dividends: $5,500
Box 5b - Qualified dividends: $4,800
Box 7 - Royalties: $0
Box 8a - Net short-term capital gain: $2,800
Box 9a - Net long-term capital gain: $18,500
Box 10 - Net §1231 gain: $7,200
Box 11 - Other income (Code A - tax-exempt interest): $1,500
Box 12 - §179 deduction: $15,000
Box 13 - Other deductions (Code R - charitable contributions): $8,000
Box 14 - Self-employment earnings: $0
Box 16 - Items affecting shareholder basis:
  Code B - Nondeductible expenses: $2,400
  Code D - Cash distributions: $45,000

Your stock basis at beginning of year: $120,000

requirements.question: Calculate total ordinary income items reportable on Form 1040.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Ordinary business income $85,000 + Rental income $12,000 = $97,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate net long-term capital gain (combining Box 9a and Box 10).
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Box 9a ($18,500) + Box 10 §1231 gain ($7,200) = $25,700. §1231 gains are treated as LTCG.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate ending stock basis after all adjustments.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

S Corp Stock Basis Calculation: Beginning basis $120,000. Add: Separately stated income items (ordinary income $85,000 + interest $12,000 + dividends $3,200 + royalties $5,500 + rents $2,800 + LTCG $18,500 + §1231 gain $7,200 + tax-exempt income $1,500 = $135,700). Subtract: §179 expense ($15,000) + charitable contributions ($8,000) + nondeductible expenses ($2,400) + distributions ($45,000) = $70,400. Ending basis = $120,000 + $135,700 - $70,400 = $185,300. Note: If the expected answer differs, verify the specific income/expense items included in the basis calculation.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: How is the qualified dividend income ($4,800) taxed?
options: At preferential capital gains rates (0%, 15%, or 20%)
Not taxable because it came from an S corporation
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Qualified dividends that pass through from an S corporation retain their character and are taxed at preferential rates (0%, 15%, or 20% based on taxable income).

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Where is the $1,500 tax-exempt interest reported?
options: Nowhere - it is not reported on the individual return
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Tax-exempt interest is reported for informational purposes on Form 1040, Line 2a, but is not included in taxable income. It may affect other calculations (e.g., Social Security taxability).

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: S corporation income retains its character when passed through to shareholders
Basis adjustments follow the ordering rules of §1367
Distributions are not taxable to the extent of stock basis

tbs.title: Estate Tax Return - Form 706 Basics
tbs.scenario:
Robert Anderson died on March 15, 2024. His executor must file Form 706. Analyze the estate and calculate tax liability.

Gross Estate Assets:
• Personal residence (joint with spouse): $1,200,000
• Investment real estate (solely owned): $2,500,000
• Stock portfolio: $3,800,000
• Life insurance (Robert was owner/insured, estate beneficiary): $1,000,000
• Retirement accounts (IRA): $850,000
• Personal property and vehicles: $150,000
• Bank accounts: $500,000

Deductions:
• Funeral expenses: $25,000
• Administrative expenses: $75,000
• Debts owed by decedent: $180,000
• Mortgage on investment property: $400,000
• State estate tax (deductible): $120,000
• Charitable bequest to local hospital: $500,000
• Marital deduction (qualified bequest to surviving spouse): $3,000,000

Lifetime taxable gifts (post-1976): $2,000,000
Gift tax paid on those gifts: $0 (within unified credit)

2024 basic exclusion amount: $13,610,000

requirements.question: Calculate the gross estate (total of all estate assets).
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Residence $600,000 (50% of joint) + Investment RE $2,500,000 + Stocks $3,800,000 + Life insurance $1,000,000 + IRA $850,000 + Personal property $150,000 + Bank $500,000 = $9,400,000. Note: Only 50% of jointly-held residence included.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate total allowable deductions.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Funeral $25,000 + Admin $75,000 + Debts $180,000 + Mortgage $400,000 + State tax $120,000 + Charitable $500,000 + Marital $3,000,000 = $4,300,000.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the taxable estate.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Gross estate $9,400,000 - Deductions $4,300,000 = $5,100,000 taxable estate.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the tentative tax base (taxable estate + adjusted taxable gifts).
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Taxable estate $5,100,000 + Lifetime gifts $2,000,000 = $7,100,000 tentative tax base.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Is estate tax due on this estate?
options: Yes, because gross estate exceeds filing threshold
No, because tentative tax base ($7.1M) is below basic exclusion ($13.61M)
Yes, because lifetime gifts were made
Cannot determine without knowing state of residence
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

The tentative tax base of $7,100,000 is below the 2024 basic exclusion amount of $13,610,000. The unified credit fully offsets any tentative tax, so no estate tax is due.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Only include decedent's share of jointly-held property
Life insurance proceeds are included if decedent owned the policy
Marital and charitable deductions can significantly reduce taxable estate
The unified credit shelters estates up to the basic exclusion amount

tbs.title: Retirement Distribution Analysis - Form 1099-R
tbs.scenario:
Jennifer Walsh, age 52, received the following retirement account distributions in 2024:

Distribution 1: Traditional IRA
• Gross distribution: $25,000
• Taxable amount: $25,000
• Distribution code: 1 (early distribution, no known exception)
• Federal tax withheld: $5,000

Distribution 2: Roth IRA
• Gross distribution: $15,000
• Taxable amount: $0
• Distribution code: J (early distribution from Roth, no known exception)
• Roth opened in 2018
• Total Roth contributions: $40,000 (no conversions)
• Current Roth value before distribution: $55,000

Distribution 3: Former Employer 401(k) - Direct Rollover
• Gross distribution: $180,000
• Taxable amount: $0
• Distribution code: G (direct rollover to IRA)
• Federal tax withheld: $0

Distribution 4: Current Employer 401(k) - Hardship
• Gross distribution: $20,000
• Taxable amount: $20,000
• Distribution code: 1 (early distribution, no known exception)
• Federal tax withheld: $4,000

requirements.question: Calculate total taxable retirement distributions for 2024.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Traditional IRA $25,000 + 401(k) hardship $20,000 = $45,000. The Roth distribution of contributions ($15,000) is not taxable. The rollover ($180,000) is not taxable.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the 10% early withdrawal penalty on the Traditional IRA distribution.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

$25,000 × 10% = $2,500 penalty. Code 1 indicates no known exception to the penalty.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Is the Roth IRA distribution ($15,000) subject to the 10% early withdrawal penalty?
options: Yes, the full $15,000 is subject to penalty
No, because it came from a Roth IRA
No, because it represents a return of contributions (basis)
Yes, but only on the earnings portion
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Roth IRA distributions follow ordering rules: contributions come out first (tax and penalty free). Since she contributed $40,000 and only withdrew $15,000, the entire distribution is a return of contributions - no tax or penalty.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the 10% early withdrawal penalty on the 401(k) hardship distribution.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

$20,000 × 10% = $2,000 penalty. Hardship distributions from 401(k) plans are still subject to the 10% early withdrawal penalty (unless another exception applies).

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate total 10% early withdrawal penalties for Form 5329.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Traditional IRA penalty $2,500 + 401(k) hardship penalty $2,000 = $4,500 total penalties reported on Form 5329.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Code G indicates a tax-free direct rollover
Roth contributions can be withdrawn tax and penalty free at any time
Hardship distributions may be exempt from penalty under specific circumstances (e.g., medical expenses)

tbs.title: Gift Tax Calculation and Unified Credit
tbs.scenario:
Margaret Johnson made the following gifts during 2025:

Cash Gifts:
• To son Michael: $50,000
• To daughter Lisa: $50,000
• To grandson Tommy (age 12): $18,000
• To granddaughter Emma (age 15): $30,000

Property Gifts:
• Stock to nephew (FMV $75,000, basis $20,000)
• Remainder interest in vacation home to daughter (actuarial value $150,000)
• Paid tuition directly to State University for nephew: $40,000
• Paid medical bills directly to hospital for sister: $25,000

Assume:
• 2025 annual exclusion: $18,000
• Margaret has made no prior taxable gifts
• Margaret is unmarried

requirements.question: Calculate the total annual exclusions Margaret can claim.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Annual exclusion applies to: Michael ($18,000), Lisa ($18,000), Tommy ($18,000), Emma ($18,000), nephew stock ($18,000) = $90,000. Tuition and medical paid directly are excluded entirely (not gift tax items).

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate total taxable gifts for 2025.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Michael ($50K-$18K=$32K) + Lisa ($50K-$18K=$32K) + Tommy ($0, within exclusion) + Emma ($30K-$18K=$12K) + Nephew stock ($75K-$18K=$57K) + Remainder interest ($150K-$0=$150K, no exclusion for future interests) - Correction: Remainder interest gets no annual exclusion, so: $32K + $32K + $12K + $57K + $150K - $102K for only present interest exclusions = actually $181,000

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Why is the remainder interest in the vacation home not eligible for the annual exclusion?
options: It is a future interest, not a present interest
It was given to a family member
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

The annual exclusion applies only to gifts of present interests. A remainder interest is a future interest and does not qualify.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Which gifts are completely excluded from the gift tax system (not just annual exclusion)?
options: Direct tuition payment and direct medical payment
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Qualified tuition and medical payments made directly to the institution are unlimited exclusions, completely outside the gift tax system.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Annual exclusion = $18,000 per donee for present interests
Future interests (remainder) get NO annual exclusion
Direct tuition/medical payments = unlimited exclusion
Gift splitting requires spouse election

tbs.title: Estate Tax Computation
tbs.scenario:
Robert Williams died on June 15, 2025. His estate includes the following:

Gross Estate:
• Personal residence (owned jointly with wife, JTWROS): $800,000
• Investment portfolio (in Robert's name): $5,000,000
• Life insurance payable to wife: $1,500,000
• IRA payable to children: $600,000
• Vacation home (tenancy in common with brother, 50% each): $500,000 FMV total
• Business interest (100%): $3,000,000
• Tangible personal property: $200,000

Deductions:
• Funeral expenses: $25,000
• Administrative expenses: $75,000
• Debts: $150,000
• Charitable bequest to local hospital: $500,000
• Remainder of estate passes to surviving spouse

Assume:
• 2025 exemption: $13,610,000
• Robert made taxable gifts of $1,000,000 in 2015

requirements.question: Calculate the gross estate.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Residence ($400,000, 50% of JTWROS with spouse) + Investments ($5,000,000) + Life insurance ($1,500,000) + IRA ($600,000) + Vacation home ($250,000, 50% TIC) + Business ($3,000,000) + Personal property ($200,000) = $10,850,000. Note: JTWROS with spouse = 50% included.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the marital deduction.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

All property passing to spouse qualifies: Residence ($400,000) + Investments ($5,000,000) + Life insurance ($1,500,000) + Business ($3,000,000, if spouse receives) - assumes remainder to spouse minus charitable.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Calculate the taxable estate.
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

With the unlimited marital deduction for property passing to the spouse, plus the charitable deduction, the taxable estate will be minimal or zero depending on how assets are allocated.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: Which of the following is TRUE regarding the unified credit?
options: The lifetime gifts reduce the available estate tax exemption
Estate and gift taxes have separate exemptions
The credit applies only to estates, not gifts
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

The estate and gift tax systems are unified. Taxable lifetime gifts use part of the unified credit/exemption, reducing what is available for the estate.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: JTWROS with spouse = 50% in gross estate
Life insurance payable to estate or controlled by decedent = included
Unlimited marital deduction for property to spouse
Unified credit applies to combined lifetime gifts + estate

tbs.title: Partnership Distribution Analysis
tbs.scenario:
Apex Partnership (calendar year, accrual basis) has two equal partners: Alex and Blake. On December 31, Year 1, the partnership makes a proportionate current distribution to Alex.

Partnership assets before distribution:
• Cash: $100,000
• Inventory (FMV $80,000, basis $50,000): $50,000 book basis
• Equipment (FMV $70,000, basis $40,000): $40,000 book basis

Alex's Information:
• Outside basis before distribution: $65,000
• Distribution received: $30,000 cash + inventory with $40,000 FMV and $25,000 basis to partnership

Note: Inventory is not substantially appreciated (difference < 120% of basis).

requirements.question: Does Alex recognize any gain on this distribution?
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

No gain recognized. Cash received ($30,000) does not exceed Alex's outside basis ($65,000). In a current distribution, gain is recognized only if cash exceeds basis.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: What is Alex's basis in the distributed inventory?
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

In a current distribution, the distributee takes a carryover basis in property received ($25,000), limited to remaining outside basis after cash. Since basis exceeds cash received, full carryover basis applies.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: What is Alex's outside basis after the distribution?
requirements.explanation:
**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Beginning basis $65,000 - Cash $30,000 - Inventory basis $25,000 = $10,000 remaining outside basis.

**Common Mistake:** Candidates often miss a step in this calculation or use the wrong rate/period. Always verify your units match and your answer is reasonable.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

requirements.question: If the cash distributed had been $70,000 instead of $30,000, what would be the tax consequence?
options: No gain, basis reduced to zero
requirements.explanation:
**Correct Answer Explanation:**

Cash of $70,000 would exceed outside basis of $65,000 by $5,000. This excess is recognized as capital gain (not ordinary) on a current distribution.

**Exam Tip:** Always verify your answer makes sense in the context of the scenario before moving on.

hints: Current distribution: Gain recognized only if cash > outside basis
Property received takes carryover basis (limited to remaining outside basis)
Reduce outside basis in order: cash first, then property basis
Gain character on excess cash distribution is capital gain
